lesson.name: Plato: Dialogues & the Republic - Lesson 1 — The Apology

meta.lessonObjective: Socrates' defence and the examined life.

tasks.chapterTitle: Historical Context and Misconceptions of Socrates
The Trial: Socrates' Defense Against Meletus
The Aftermath and Reflections on Death

messages.content:
Lesson 1 — The Apology - Presentation25 Slides
The Nature of Socrates' Defence
  • The accusers have spoken persuasively but with scarcely a word of truth, and Socrates declares he will speak the whole truth in his accustomed manner rather than in set orations.
  • He asks the judges to attend not to the manner of his speech but only to the truth of his words and to decide justly according to the law.
Test your understanding of this lesson:

questions.question: According to Socrates, what did the Delphic oracle declare about him?
options: That he was the wisest of men
That no one was wiser than he
That he should cease examining others
That he would be condemned
questions.explanation: The oracle answered that no one was wiser than Socrates, prompting his mission of examination.

questions.question: Why does Socrates say he is wiser than the politicians and poets he examined?
options: Because he knew many fine arts
Because he did not claim to know what he did not know
Because the god commanded him to teach them
Because he had studied their writings
questions.explanation: He knew he knew nothing, while they imagined they knew what they did not.

questions.question: What metaphor does Socrates use for his role in Athens?
options: A horse trainer
A gadfly attached to the state
A physician of the soul
A captain of the ship of state
questions.explanation: He is the gadfly sent by God to stir the noble but sluggish steed of the state.

questions.question: What does Socrates say will happen if the Athenians kill him?
options: They will injure him greatly
They will injure themselves more than him
The oracle will be proved false
Young men will stop examining others
questions.explanation: A bad man cannot injure a better one; the loss falls on Athens.

questions.question: What conclusion does Socrates draw from the silence of his divine sign on the day of trial?
options: That death is certainly an evil
That what has happened is a good
That the judges will acquit him
That he should have prepared a defence
questions.explanation: The sign's silence indicates death is not the evil men suppose.

cards.front: What mission did the Delphic oracle give Socrates?
cards.back: To examine those reputed wise and show that human wisdom is worth little or nothing.

cards.front: Why did Socrates approach the poets?
cards.back: To test whether they were wiser than he by asking the meaning of their own verses.

cards.front: What discovery did Socrates make about artisans?
cards.back: They knew their crafts but wrongly supposed they also knew all high matters.

cards.front: How does Socrates describe his relation to the god?
cards.back: He obeys God rather than men and remains at the post where the god has placed him.

cards.front: What is the gadfly metaphor?
cards.back: Socrates is the gadfly sent by God to arouse the sluggish state into examining its life.

cards.front: Why did the divine sign never oppose Socrates on the day of trial?
cards.back: Its silence showed that death is a good, not an evil to be feared.

cards.front: What two possibilities for death does Socrates present?
cards.back: Either dreamless sleep or migration of the soul to another place; in either case death is a gain.

cards.front: What does Socrates refuse to do at his trial?
cards.back: He refuses to weep, beg, or present his children to win pity from the judges.

cards.front: What prophecy does Socrates make to his condemners?
cards.back: Younger accusers will arise and punish them more severely for failing to examine their lives.

cards.front: What is the central activity of the examined life in the Apology?
cards.back: Detecting pretended wisdom by questioning those who claim to know.

slides.title: The Nature of Socrates' Defence
bullets: The accusers have spoken persuasively but with scarcely a word of truth, and Socrates declares he will speak the whole truth in his accustomed manner rather than in set orations.
He asks the judges to attend not to the manner of his speech but only to the truth of his words and to decide justly according to the law.

This plain manner of speaking therefore forces the judges to confront the case without the familiar comfort of rhetorical ornament, exposing how easily Athenian courts can mistake eloquence for evidence.

By placing the entire weight of decision on the truth alone, Socrates creates a lasting tension between the philosopher's duty to speak honestly and the city's habit of rewarding those who know how to please their listeners.
slides.notes: Sets the tone of defiant plain speech from the opening.

slides.title: Two Classes of Accusers
bullets: Socrates must first answer the older accusers who have filled the minds of Athenians since childhood with the slander that he speculates about the heavens and makes the worse appear the better cause.
These ancient slanders are more dangerous because their hearers were impressionable in youth and no one answered them at the time.

Because these prejudices took root before any defense could be offered, Socrates now faces a jury whose very capacity to hear evidence has already been shaped against him, making ordinary persuasion nearly impossible.

The older slanders therefore do more than damage reputation; they turn the entire city into an unwitting ally of the present prosecutors, so that the trial becomes a contest not merely over facts but over whether philosophy itself can still be heard in Athens.

slides.title: The Origin of the Slander
bullets: The affidavit of the old accusers states that Socrates is an evil-doer and curious person who searches into things under the earth and in heaven and teaches others the same doctrines.
This charge arises from Socrates' lifelong mission to understand the meaning of the Delphic oracle that declared no one wiser than he.

Because the oracle drove him to test every reputed wise man in Athens, his public questioning exposed ignorance at every level of society and left a trail of humiliated citizens eager to retaliate with rumor.

The very act of examining others therefore appeared to ordinary Athenians as arrogant meddling with divine and natural secrets, so the old prejudice hardened into a formal charge long before any new accusers appeared.
slides.notes: Links the prejudice directly to the examined life.

slides.title: The Delphic Oracle and the Examined Life
bullets: Chaerephon asked the oracle whether any man was wiser than Socrates, and the Pythian priestess answered that no one was wiser.
Socrates, knowing he possessed no wisdom, set out to test the oracle by examining those reputed wise, thereby beginning his divine mission of inquiry.
Through these encounters Socrates found that those most confident in their knowledge were often the most ignorant, exposing a widespread failure to examine the grounds of belief.

The oracle thus compelled him to persist in public questioning as a sacred duty, turning private awareness of ignorance into a lifelong practice that challenged the unexamined claims of his fellow citizens.
slides.notes: The oracle launches the examined life.

slides.title: Examining the Politicians
bullets: Socrates went first to a politician reputed wise, yet found that the man imagined he knew much while knowing little or nothing.
In this encounter Socrates concluded he was wiser than the politician because he did not claim to know what he did not know.
This encounter exposed the perilous gap between a leader's reputation and his actual understanding, showing how unexamined confidence in the powerful can mislead an entire city.
By persisting in such examinations Socrates accepted the risk of making enemies among the influential, a direct consequence that would shape both his trial and his defense of the examined life.

slides.title: Examining the Poets
bullets: Socrates next approached the tragic and dithyrambic poets and asked them the meaning of their own elaborate passages, discovering that they could not explain them.
The poets, like diviners, spoke many fine things through inspiration but understood nothing of what they said, and therefore believed themselves wise in matters where they were not.

This encounter shows that inspiration can produce striking verses while leaving their authors unable to account for the very truths they seem to utter, so the poets remain trapped in an unexamined confidence that blocks any path to real understanding.

By exposing this gap Socrates illustrates why the examined life must test even our most admired gifts, because without such scrutiny inspiration breeds not wisdom but the deeper error of mistaking beautiful speech for settled knowledge.

slides.title: Examining the Artisans
bullets: Socrates turned finally to the artisans, who knew many fine things he did not know and were therefore wiser in their crafts.
Yet even the good artisans fell into the error of supposing that because they excelled in their own art they also knew all high matters, and this conceit overshadowed their wisdom.
This presumption leads them to pronounce on justice, virtue, and the good of the city with an authority their craft alone cannot supply, thereby misleading both themselves and their fellow citizens.

The resulting blindness prevents any further inquiry into the foundations of their own knowledge, so that technical mastery, instead of opening the way to wisdom, becomes the very obstacle that keeps the artisans from examining their lives.

slides.title: The Result of the Mission
bullets: The outcome of Socrates' inquiry was that those most in repute were nearly the most foolish, while others less esteemed proved wiser and better.
God alone is wise, and the oracle used Socrates' name to show that human wisdom is worth little or nothing.

This discovery forces Socrates to keep questioning every claim to knowledge, turning his daily conversations into a public service that strips away false confidence and leaves people more aware of what they truly lack.

The result is a life of constant examination that honors the god by refusing to let reputation stand in place of understanding, even when that refusal provokes the very people whose good opinion once seemed valuable.
slides.notes: Core finding of the examined life.

slides.title: The Divine Mission Continues
bullets: Socrates goes about obedient to the god, searching into the wisdom of any who appear wise and showing them they are not, in vindication of the oracle.
This occupation absorbs him completely, leaving no time for public affairs or private concerns and resulting in utter poverty.
Because this search is commanded by the god it cannot be set aside even when it collides with the city's expectations of civic duty and family provision.
The resulting poverty and isolation therefore become the visible cost of keeping the examined life alive in Athens rather than allowing its citizens to rest in untested claims to wisdom.
slides.notes: The examined life as lifelong duty.

slides.title: Young Men Imitate the Examination
bullets: Young men of the richer sort, having leisure, delight in hearing pretenders to wisdom detected and begin to imitate Socrates by examining others themselves.
Those exposed then grow angry not with themselves but with Socrates, repeating the stock charges that he teaches things in the clouds and has no gods.
This imitation multiplies examiners across the city, so that every fresh exposure adds another voice to the chorus of resentment while Socrates remains the single visible target.

The unintended result is that the examined life, once taken up by eager followers, begins to generate its own enemies and thereby hastens the day when Socrates must answer for the disturbance in court.

slides.title: The Charge of Corrupting the Youth
bullets: Meletus accuses Socrates of corrupting the youth, yet when asked who improves the citizens he answers that all men do, an answer Socrates shows to be absurd by analogy with other living beings.
Socrates argues that if he corrupts the youth he must do so unintentionally, in which case Meletus should have instructed rather than indicted him.

Such a claim collapses once we notice that improvement in any craft belongs only to those who possess the relevant knowledge, so Meletus unwittingly confesses he has no art by which the young could be made better.

To prosecute rather than correct an unwitting error therefore exposes the accuser’s own refusal to examine his beliefs, turning the courtroom into a place where fear of questioning overrides any real care for the souls of the young.

slides.title: The Charge of Atheism Examined
bullets: The indictment claims Socrates does not believe in the gods of the state but introduces new divinities, yet Meletus also asserts Socrates believes in no gods at all.
Socrates exposes the contradiction that one cannot believe in the sons of gods while disbelieving in gods themselves.

This contradiction forces the jury to confront how an unexamined accusation can collapse under its own weight, turning the trial into a living test of whether Athens values consistency over prejudice.

By revealing that the charges rest on ignorance rather than inquiry, Socrates shows that genuine reverence for the divine demands the same rigorous self-scrutiny he practices, lest the city punish its own conscience.
slides.notes: Second part of the formal charge.

slides.title: Obedience to God Rather Than Men
bullets: Socrates declares he will obey God rather than man and will continue to preach the necessity of virtue even if a thousand deaths await him.
He will not cease his examination of others because the god has placed him at this post, just as he remained at his military posts at Potidaea, Amphipolis, and Delium.

This stance turns every conversation into a test of loyalty to the divine, where yielding to human threats would mean betraying the very source of his mission and leaving his fellow citizens asleep in their unexamined opinions.

The military parallel shows that fear of death cannot override an assigned station, so Socrates treats the marketplace itself as a battlefield where retreat would disgrace both the god and the soul that refuses to examine itself.

slides.title: The Gadfly of the State
bullets: Socrates describes himself as a gadfly sent by God to the great and noble steed of the state, which is tardy in its motions and needs constant stirring into life.
He fastens upon citizens everywhere, arousing, persuading, and reproaching them, and warns that they will not easily find another such.
If the state succeeds in driving away or silencing this divine irritant, it will sink back into sluggish complacency and repeat the very errors that once threatened its survival.

The gadfly’s sting therefore carries a double edge: it awakens individual citizens to their own unexamined beliefs while simultaneously placing the entire city under a painful obligation to justify its actions before it acts again.
slides.notes: Famous metaphor for the examined life.

slides.title: The Divine Sign That Deters
bullets: A divine voice or sign has come to Socrates since childhood, always forbidding but never commanding what he is about to do, and this sign has kept him from entering public life.
Had he engaged in politics he would have perished long ago, for no man who honestly fights the many lawless deeds of a multitude can save his life.
By heeding this inner restraint Socrates preserves the freedom to examine individual souls in private conversation rather than risk silencing himself through an early death in the assembly.

The sign therefore creates a quiet tension between personal safety and civic duty, allowing one honest man to continue his questioning only by standing apart from the very institutions that claim to serve justice.
slides.notes: The daimonion as protector of integrity.

slides.title: Standing Against Injustice Twice
bullets: As the only senator who opposed the illegal trial of the generals after Arginusae, Socrates voted against the proposal despite threats of impeachment and arrest.
Under the Thirty he refused to fetch Leon of Salamis for execution and went home quietly while the others obeyed, showing he would not participate in unrighteous acts.
These refusals placed personal safety and civic standing at risk under two opposed regimes, showing that moral consistency could isolate a citizen from every faction at once.
The same pattern of principled withdrawal later supplied the chief accusation at his trial, where Athens treated such independence itself as a threat to the city.

slides.title: Refusal to Alter His Ways
bullets: Socrates tells the court he will never alter his ways even if he must die many times, because he is certain that desertion of his divine duty is an evil while he does not know whether death is good or evil.
He will not entreat the judges or present weeping children, for such conduct would discredit Athens and violate the judge's oath to give justice.

This stance compels the jurors to confront their own fear of death and their willingness to punish integrity rather than examine their lives, exposing a tension between civic comfort and the pursuit of truth.

By accepting the unknown outcome of death rather than abandoning his mission Socrates illustrates that the examined life must remain consistent even when it leads to condemnation, leaving the city with an unresolved moral example that outlasts any single verdict.
slides.notes: Moral resolve at the penalty stage.

slides.title: The Counter-Penalty Proposed
bullets: Socrates proposes maintenance in the Prytaneum as the fitting reward for his benefactions, since he has spent his life improving the citizens without fee.
He refuses to propose imprisonment or exile because he does not know whether death is an evil and is certain that those alternatives are evils.

This demand for public upkeep therefore places the jury in the awkward position of having to decide whether the city truly values the very activity that keeps its citizens awake to their own ignorance.

By rejecting every alternative that would let him continue his questioning elsewhere or in secret, Socrates makes clear that the examined life must be lived openly in the same place where it began, even when that choice seals his fate.

slides.title: Death Not an Evil to Fear
bullets: Socrates argues that a bad man is not permitted to injure a better one, so neither Meletus nor Anytus can truly harm him.
The real injury would fall on the Athenians if they kill their heaven-sent examiner and lose the chance to improve their lives.
By attempting to silence their examiner through death the Athenians only deepen their own moral blindness and forfeit the daily self-scrutiny that alone improves a life.
Socrates therefore faces execution without fear because the harm they intend rebounds upon their own characters while his integrity remains intact.

slides.title: Prophecy to the Condemners
bullets: Immediately after Socrates' death, punishment heavier than death will come upon those who condemned him, for younger accusers whom he had restrained will now assail them without restraint.
Killing critics will not silence examination of evil lives; the only honourable escape is to improve oneself.
Their act of silencing one examiner will therefore multiply the very scrutiny they sought to escape, as the young now press questions without the old restraint that once tempered public exposure.
In this way the city itself inherits an unexamined wound that festers, since only the hard work of self-correction, not the removal of critics, can restore its health.

slides.title: The Silence of the Divine Sign
bullets: Throughout the day of trial the accustomed sign gave no opposition to anything Socrates said or did, although it had often stopped him in the middle of a speech before.
This silence indicates that what has happened is a good, not the worst evil men suppose death to be.
The divine silence therefore transforms the verdict itself into an endorsement, allowing Socrates to meet his end without the inner check that once preserved him from lesser errors of speech or deed.

This absence of warning compels his companions to recognize that philosophy, stripped of its accustomed guardian, must now be sustained by the same unaided reason that first invited the sign to speak.
slides.notes: Evidence that death is not evil.

slides.title: Two Possibilities for Death
bullets: Death is either a state of nothingness and dreamless sleep, in which case it is an unspeakable gain because eternity becomes a single night better than most days of life, or else a migration of the soul to another place.
In either case death cannot be an evil, and the man who fears it while doing no wrong mistakes the true object of fear, which is unrighteousness.
This outlook therefore removes the usual excuse for compromising one's principles under pressure, since neither possibility grants any lasting advantage to the unjust man over the just.
The examined life thus acquires a new steadiness, for the citizen who has already confronted these two prospects can weigh every action solely by whether it increases or lessens unrighteousness.

slides.title: The Examined Life as Divine Service
bullets: Socrates has passed his life as a missionary detecting the pretended wisdom of mankind because the god's word must be considered first.
He will persist in this service to the end, for the unexamined life is the opposite of the mission laid upon him by the oracle.

By placing the god's command above every human claim, Socrates turns daily conversation into an act of worship that strips away false certainties and leaves only the humble awareness that wisdom belongs to the divine alone.

This lifelong obedience creates an irreconcilable tension with ordinary civic life, since the same questioning that fulfills the oracle inevitably provokes resentment and danger, yet to stop would be to choose a hollow existence over sacred duty.
